title: Mookupodi Swamigal
meta_description: Mookupodi Swamigal (born Mottayan Gaunder, d. December 9, 2018) was an enigmatic siddhar who lived on the streets of Tiruvannamalai for over thirty years, famous for his unorthodox behaviour and blessings.
body_html:
Life of Mookupodi Swami

The famed Mookupodi Swamigal (Siddhar) who devoted his life to prayer and seclusion, passed away at Seshadri Ashram, Tiruvannamalai, on Sunday morning at 5.30 a.m., December 9, 2018. His death came nearly ten days after he had fallen whilst moving around Arunachala.

Mookupodi Swamigal spent his life on the streets, roads, shops, ashrams, temples, mandapams — at the foot of Arunachala. He frequently wore a green shawl and would regularly inhale tobacco powder — hence his nomenclature "Mookupodi". He rarely spoke and it was believed that if he did speak or look at a person's face, he was giving his blessings.

Mookupodi Swamigal was born as Mottayan Gaunder in his family's ancestral home on Shivan Kovil Street, East Rajapalayam, near Salem. During his youth he would spend most of his time at the Veerapathiran Temple. At the age of 25, his family arranged his marriage to a girl named Chadachi and the couple had a son named Periyaswamy.

Shortly after Periyaswamy's birth, Mottayan Gaunder left his birth village and began his spiritual journey as a travelling Sadhu. After wandering for 12 years he returned for the passing away of his wife, then continued his wandering life. He eventually arrived at Tiruvannamalai where, over time, he became known as Mookupodi Swamigal.

Old photograph of Mottayan Gaunder

Periyaswamy, son of Swamigal

During his time at Tiruvannamalai he lived at a number of Temples including Hanuman Temple, Rajarajeshwari Temple, Adi Annamalai Temple, Karumariamman Temple and towards the end of his life at the Navagraha Shrine on Girivalam Road next to Unnamulai Teertham. Increasingly large crowds of devotees gathered to take his darshan.

Mookupodi Siddhar behaved in unorthodox and unusual ways. He would shout at and chase people and sometimes even beat some with his lathi — believed to be both a blessing and a teaching. By the time of his death he was regularly visited by local and State politicians as well as devotees from across Tamil Nadu.

Devotees Stories of Mookupodi Siddhar

December 2007 — Went for a Meal and met a Saint
I was taking meals at the old Hotel Deepam on Car Street when I first saw Mottayan Swamiji. He was sitting on top of a box style ice cream freezer with his back leaning against the wall — motionless and very dirty, but there was something about him that peaked my curiosity. It is believed he moved to Tiruvannamalai over 30 years ago. He would not know of sleep or waking; his eyes were almost always absorbed inward. Wherever he remained it was always with the grateful support of the owner, who believed Swami's presence blessed their business, health and life.

Swamiji in 2007 at Hotel Deepam

Mookupodi Swamigal at Navagraha Shrine, Girivalam Roadway

Samadhi

The famed Mookupodi Swamigal passed away at Seshadri Ashram, Tiruvannamalai, on Sunday morning at 5.30 a.m., December 9, 2018. His body was entombed in Samadhi in a dedicated place opposite the Vayu Lingam on the Girivalam Roadway.
notes: No hero image. Very long page with many devotee testimonials. Testimonials condensed to first one; all images captured.
